sentences.text: Another thing that was present on my mind, and that I had learned through experience, is to avoid getting on the list.
Once your name is on a list, that list acquires holy status.
It cannot be tampered with.
It's a document by which everybody lives.
Everybody who administers these people, and that situation, lives by that list.
You cannot cross out a name.
You have to justify it.
And et cetera.
So I judged the moment was right after the officer had spoken, and I approached him.
I said, excuse me, please.
I said, I studied a little German.
I speak it very badly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But purposely making grammatical mistakes and so forth, I said, I'm merely inquiring whether, by chance, there might not be a mistake here, because I'm only 17-- 17 years old.
I'm a theology student at the Catholic orphanage, and I don't know what I'm doing here.
And I showed him the false identity card given to me by Don Giovanni.
He looks at the card.
He looks at me.
He looks at the card.
I had on some heavy boots given to me by the orphanage, like everything else was given to me by the orphanage.
He looks at me, and he says, you're only 17?
Big guy like-- like you?
I said, hey, it's not my fault, you should've seen mom.
So that kind of amused him, but didn't convince him yet.
He said, and these boots?
That's army issue, right?
I said, no, no, no.
This was given to me by the orphanage.
We make do with what they have.
We can't go and buy shoes.
After a moment of reflection, he folded the card, gave it back to me, and said, now go and stand by the open door there.
Outside it was summer.
It was a glaring sunlight.
I closed my eyes.
He said, now turn around-- so that's when I was facing the bright, glaring landscape out-- outside.
I'm now facing this open door, and the white-washed landscape on the outside.
I don't know what's going on in back of me.
I suddenly hear very strong, fast, heavy running steps.
And before I knew it, he had kicked me in the behind with such force that I was flying over the threshold in an arc outside and landed in the dust.
I straightened myself out.
I looked at him.
He said, you're lucky.
Don't let me catch-- catch you again.
You-- you may go.
And I said danke schoen, danke schoen.
And I slowly walked away.
I didn't want to show the haste with which I was happy to be out of their clutches.
And I started making my way back to the orphanage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was in a delirium thinking that I owed my life to that fake identity card and to that one-year-younger that Don Giovanni had managed, as a precaution, to type in there.
So I was floating through the air.
I was free.
I was out of the German clutches again, walking back towards the orphanage by a side road.
I didn't want to go back to the road where the soldiers had come up.
And in the distance, I see, coming towards me, a stooped little figure and a small child.
I couldn't make out who they were yet.
But as we came closer to each other, I realized it was Don Fascimeni, the director of the orphanage.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was caught on the way to the farmhouse, the German-- one of the two accompanying us there-- went into abandoned houses along the way, and came out with what-- with whatever he could pilfer.
At one time, he came out with a rabbit in a bag.
And he gave me the rabbit to be carried on my shoulder.
And that rabbit was digging frantically its nails into my-- my-- my back.
So I now encounter Don Julio Fascimeni with that little boy.
He said, oh, my god.
Have they let you go?
I said, yes.
He said, I was in such fear for your life, because I had been told that you were caught with the others and that you were carrying a bag.
And I thought that these were bags of weapons they had found stashed away in the orphanage.
So I was sure they were going to execute you.
So I was on my way there now, he said, to offer myself in exchange for your life.
Now, you must remember, I haven't spoken yet of Don Julio Fascimeni.
He was a man of infinite goodness.
And throughout my stay at the orphanage, we developed a close relationship.
Perhaps because he was empathizing with my situation as a Jewish refugee in a Catholic orphanage.
He was wonderful to all the boys, but especially to me and to Willy.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So that this gesture of him going to exchange himself was in keeping with his attitude.
And we had developed a close relationship to the point that he was also a very sick man.
He had Parkinson's disease.
He walked in the hesitating little steps.
He had to be helped by somebody else all the time, so this trip for him, all the way to where we were was, quite an ordeal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The relationship with him during the orphanage-- my stay at the orphanage became very close and very warm.
I even was [CHUCKLES] promoted to his personal barber.
So twice a week, it was my job to shave him.
And I was very conscious of this situation, because he was highly respected, not just at the orphanage, naturally, and in the area of Florence where we were-- Rifredi-- but all throughout Florence.
He was considered a saint.
And I was conscious of this situation where a Jewish refugee boy shaving an eminent priest of the Catholic Church in a very cozy relationship.
So I made my way back to the orphanage.
He went on to the farmhouse to see if he could retrieve some of the other boys.
But he was told, don't worry.
They're going to work in factories in German-- in Germany.
They'll be safe.
Fact is that only three of them returned-- of this entire group-- later.
I remained in the orphanage with Willy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until the liberation of Florence, which happened-- Florence being considered a special case because of its art treasures.
As the Germans retreated, they blew up all the bridges across the Arno River except the famous Ponte Vecchio, which goes back to the 17th century.
But they blew up the houses on both sides of the famous bridge so it could not be used.
We at the orphanage [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had heard the cannons all throughout the nights and so forth, but we had not seen any Allied troops yet.
And one orphanage kid came back from the city.
He said, I was in the center, and there are British soldiers there.
That's all I needed.
Salvation so near, and yet not here.
What do we do?
Do we stay here and wait until they pass the orphanage?
Or do we try to make our-- our way into the city?
We decided we would go, Willy and I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We said our good-- goodbyes.
We left the orphanage.
The door closed behind us, the gate.
We are now in the street-- totally deserted.
Now, this was no man's land.
There were still Germans in our area, but we didn't know how close the Allied troops were.

We decided we would go towards the center of town, so we walked down the main street, turned, and crossed a small bridge that had been blown up, and found ourselves on the main square of that section of Florence-- Rifredi-- Piazza Dalmazia.
Totally deserted.
Eerie silence.
Only now and then you heard a machine gun, a rifle.
No people.
Nothing.
Now we had to cross this entire empty space of the square, but we didn't dare.
So we hid behind a-- a column, and we watched.
Over there, across the square, behind a newspaper kiosk, we saw some movement but couldn't determine whether it was a civilian, an Italian, a German, an Allied, nothing.
So we decided to wait and-- and see.
Now and then, that person behind the newspaper kiosk betrayed himself by a slight movement.
We could see the wheel of a bicycle.
That person behind the newspaper kiosk must have judged at some time, it's safe to come out.
So he came out pushing the bicycle.
And we both look, Willy and I, and we said, is it?
It couldn't, be.
Yes, it is.
Don Giovanni Semioni.
Having made his way all the way from the Varlungo Convent to us to see if we were safe.
And when we recognized him, we fell into each other's arm.
He threw the bicycle down.
He said, are you crazy?
He said, do you know where you are?
You're right in the middle of no man's land.
I said, what about you?
The same thing.
He said, come, quickly.
At the other end of the square was a barricade manned by partisans.
So they let us pass, and we now arrive in Florence, liberated.
And I met my first British soldier-- the symbol of life, liberty, freedom after five years of living under the Germans.
And I didn't know the guy, obviously.
Never met him.
Total stranger, but I went right up to him and kissed him.
He felt a little bit embarrassed, but by now they had been greeted with such affection all throughout Italy as they liberated.
I was adopted in liberated Florence by a unit of the Jewish brigade.
You may have heard that after resisting the formation of a Jewish fighting core from-- from Israel for a long time, the British finally gave-- gave in.
Britain, at the time, was administering Palestine.
It was on the British Mandate.
And the reason they refused the formation of a Jewish fighting unit from Israel is because they didn't want, later on, to hear claims to Jewish statehood because they fought alongside the Allies.
So they resisted as long as possible, but ultimately gave in.
And this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] section of the Jewish brigade was attached to the Eighth Army, British.
And their job was water tanks and repairs of damaged military vehicles.

So they went all through Florence distributing water to the civilian population, and they took over a garage where all the jeeps and another military vehicles from the front came back damaged and had to be fixed.
I was adopted by them.
I slept with them.
I worked with them.
I ate with-- with-- with-- with them.
When the orphanage was liberated, we went back, of course, to visit and all that.
And the kids where amazed to hear that we were not what we said we were, with names totally different.
Not French, not Italian, not even Catholics.
To [INAUDIBLE], we were Jews.
They couldn't get this.
It didn't sink in.
This was so-- they weren't convinced.
One of them said, so you speak other languages, too, right?
I said, yes.
I said, even Yiddish.
Say something in Yiddish.
Say something in-- in Yiddish.
So I said, well, I'm glad I have lived this day to be able to talk to you like-- like that, which they didn't understand.
But the strange-sounding words convinced them we were other than what they thought they were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remained with that military unit until it became known that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the British Mandate authorities would now allow Jews to go to Israel.
I have to backtrack a little bit.
At the beginning of the war, Jewish immigration to Israel was restricted by the White Paper, which was a government policy to restrict Jewish immigration so as not to offend the Arab population.
And immigration to Israel was strictly limited, but they had issued a number of certificates for those people wanting to immigrate to Israel.
These certificates were blocked during the war, because obviously no one could go, come out of-- out of Europe.
Now that the war was over, a part of these certificates of immigration were released.
So I decided I would go, although I didn't know yet what had happened to my mother and to my brother in the north and the whereabouts of my father.
But I figured we would all reunite after the-- the-- the-- the war.

I was convinced that my father would survive because, all throughout the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had known a lot of hardships I didn't speak about, but hard physical labor and situations that were anything but comfortable.
And he pulled through all the time.
So my hope was that him being in need of medical attention, at least until the wound was healing, would help him survive.
So I went ahead to establish a new family life.
I didn't want-- I wanted to be as far away from Europe as possible, which was in ruins.
The xenophobia of the French still stuck in my blood.
And I wanted to start on fresh grounds where Jews would not be dependent anymore upon the whim of other authorities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hooked up again with-- Willy also came-- I hooked up again with Ziggy.
Ziggy, you remember, was the kid my age who was with me in the garrison when we carried the straw back and forth.

But he did not want to take the chance of escaping, and he was in the transport [COUGHS], excuse me, he was in the transport that left the next morning with my father, and it's from him that I heard that my father had been gassed.
It was very simple.
When they arrived off the train, there was that loading/unloading platform.
You may have seen that in countless documentaries.
Immediately, they were told anyone sick?
Needs medical attention?
And my father, stupidly, said, yes.
I have a wound.
I just need a new bandage.
Ah, OK.
To the left.
And he had never seen my father again since then.
And everybody in Auschwitz knew what that smoke meant coming out of the chimneys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In time, as the war went on, the Allies progressed north of Italy.
And ultimately, on the very last day of the war, on the day the Armistice was signed, May 8, 1945, Treviso was liberated.
But until then, to the very last day, they were under German domination.
And ultimately, my mother and brother also came and joined me in Israel.
The friendship with the priests has remained alive and warm all throughout these years, constantly.
I have stacks of their mail.
I wrote to them.
We invited them on a trip.
We all collected money from all the survivors and paid them, on two different occasions, voyage to the Holy Land.
We toured all the holy sites.
To us and to them, you know, the Catholic sites-- Bethlehem, Nazareth, the Mount of the Beatitudes, and so forth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think part of my testimony-- here I want to make a strong point and emphasize the risk these priests took, it was punishable under death to help Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So therefore, they already took great risks.
And many priests, in fact, were either imprisoned, deported, or killed.
That was one.
The other problem was that we were total strangers.
Now, these priests could have relieved their conscience by saying, we can help unfortunate people.
But we will help those that do not put us at-- at risk.
Don't forget that Italy was being torn apart by two gigantic armies facing each other, fighting on its own soil.
Bombardments daily.
Everything was being blown to-- to bits.
There were hundreds of thousands of Italians, Christians, who were bombed out of their houses, living on the roads, living in-- where ever they-- they could.
There was plenty of help they could've directed their attention to without risking their lives to help Jews.
And if you help Jews, help Italian Jews.
They speak the language.
They have connections.
We, the foreign Jews, didn't speak the language, had no money, no papers, no place to-- to live.
We were the most vulnerable of-- of all.
And the most dangerous.
This plus the fact that we had no ration cards.
We had to be fed, and food was very strictly rationed.
There were sheets with 30 or 31 numbers in it, one for each day.
And you cut off that little coupon, and you went to the grocer or to the baker, and you got your small ration.
But we had nothing.
They took all these problems on their hands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And always with compassion, not with resentment.
OK, we have to.
And not in a superficial way, either.
Here, we found you a hiding place, we've done our share.
No.
Their concern was daily, deep, personal, and constant.
And at no time did they try to convert us to Catholicism.
Now, you must remember that Catholic is a proselytizing religion.
It's missionary.
The object is to convert as many souls as possible to Catholicism.
And on the face of it, theoretically, they would have had an easy job.
Yet here we were, Jewish refugees, and conversion would've been, theoretically, easy, because we were afraid.
We were in danger of our lives.
We were immensely grateful to the people who were saving us, the Catholics, Catholic priests.
We were aware of the dangers that they were running for-- for us.
So perhaps in-- given these conditions all put together made us, perhaps, receptive to any persistent effort at conversion.
And in fact, many of these conversions did take-- take place, especially younger children who didn't know better, who had not yet had that solid traditional Judaism from their homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But our priests never did, never did.
As a matter of fact, on the contrary.
Don Fascimeni, the head of the orphanage, always tried to reaffirm my faith.
He said, don't abandon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Stay faithful to your belief and your upbringing.
And one of his most thoughtful moments when he remembered that in his library he had a small book that he wanted to give me.
And he went looking.
And he found it with great delight and gave it to me.
It was a book of Hebrew grammar translated in Italian, which he gave-- gave to me.
So I want this testimony to be also a tribute to what these priests did.
Where were we?
I'm-- I'm in Israel.
My brother and mother join me.
My father, we know now, is dead.
And that's basically where my story ends, except, as I mentioned, that we were in constant touch with these priests.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they visited us.
We visited them.
And the time came when they were honored by the government of Israel with the Medals of the Just.
It's called the Medals for The Righteous Among the Nations.

And at Yad Vashem, which is a famous institution in Jerusalem dedicated to the memorializing of the tragedy, there is a special section called the Alley of the Just where trees are planted in their names.

So there was an official ceremony where they both came to Jerusalem, and-- the medals were given to them in Rome at the Italian embassy, and that was transmitted on the Rai, the Italian television station.
In Israel, there was a ceremony at Yad Vashem by the Eternal Flame.
And then the planting of-- of the-- of the trees.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
As I said, we remained in contact constantly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until they died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was in a movie working called when I got a call here in New York at 6:00 AM one day.
And a voice I didn't know said, are you Mr.
Louis Goldman?
Yes.
He said, I'm calling from Italy, Treviso.
I have to communicate the sad news to you that Don Giovanni died.
So I immediately made arrangements-- although I was on-- part of the crew that worked on that movie-- made arrangements to go to Treviso for the funeral.
I could not not be there.
That person I had been so closely associated with.
Besides them being priests and helping us, they were wonderful people.
Congenial and friendly and helpful and charming.
And now there's no one left except me to tell about it, and I believe this is where my story ends.
Yes.
Harry and Janette Finkelstein.
They were the couple.
Of course, he was British, Jewish.
But he had lived in France all of his life, spoke perfect French.
And she-- she was a French citizen, a-- a genuine French citizen.
And I'm still in touch with-- with them.
They now live in the south of France.
To give you some updates on other people, Ziggy, the fellow who survived Auschwitz who was in the transport with my father, now lives in Los Angeles.
Willy is in Berlin, but is thinking of coming to the States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very few of the people that they helped to survive are still alive today.
A few of them-- in Canada, in New Jersey, in France, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Israel.
I came to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to improve professionally, to study new methods of color photography and color printing, which were just at the beginning at-- at the time.
And I'm going back to-- to Israel almost every year.
My mother lived there until she died a few years ago.
My brother, Harry survived, came to Israel.
And in 1948, died in the War of Liberation.
He was barely 17 years-- years old.
And you can imagine the shock to the priests who had devoted so much of their efforts and time and love to save him to come and stand by his grave in Israel.
A very traumatic experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm fortunate to have a beautiful family-- three kids.
The younger two are still in college.
The older one is a lawyer.
My mother died a few years ago in Israel.
I live and work now in the movie industry here.
I'm a photographer, professionally.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's about up-to-date.
Whoo!
My goodness.
How do you answer a heavy loaded question like-- like that on the spur of the moment?
My message?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
How they should live their lives?
I would say that the prime requisite, to me, to my mind, is decency in all dealings with other people and yourself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also wonder-- the heroic efforts of these priests-- I've often wondered if we had been in the reverse situation, would we have done what they have done?
And I hope it will never come to the test.
I hope anything like that never happens again, period.
But it is happening all throughout the world, right?
You have cruelty and injustice and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maltreatment of one group of people versus another.
You see it in the daily head-- headlines.
I don't think the world has learned anything from-- from-- from that.
Except, perhaps, to resist absolutely fascist government, centralized, one-man power.
That's where the danger is.
Tolerance.
